What we covered

  • World news: Buffett Repositions While Nvidia Faces Geopolitical Scrutiny—What Moves SPY This Week
  • SPY learning: IV Percentile vs IV Rank: Why the Percentile Tells You What Actually Matters for Premium Selling
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Tech and Discretionary Sell-Off Weighs on Index — IV Rank at 88.55 Signals Premium Opportunity
  • PM/analytics: When PMs Build Instead of Brief: The Discovery Tax of Staying Hands-Off

Summary

Berkshire Hathaway has made a significant portfolio shift, taking a $2.6 billion stake in Delta Airlines and increasing Alphabet holdings by 224%, signaling Buffett’s confidence in airline recovery and big tech despite macro headwinds. This matters because Berkshire’s moves often telegraph institutional appetite for sectors that SPY traders are still pricing cautiously. Standard Chartered is cutting over 15% of its corporate functions workforce and targeting a 20% income-per-employee boost by 2028, reflecting pressure across global financial services to improve returns in a higher-rate environment. This squeeze on banking efficiency ripples through earnings expectations for financial sector components in SPY. Nvidia’s upcoming earnings call is expected to carry outsized weight on US equities, with particular focus on whether management will address China chip restrictions and geopolitical supply chain risk following recent diplomatic tensions. Nvidia guidance directly moves mega-cap tech positioning and therefore SPY’s large-cap weighting. India has seen fuel prices rise again—about 90 paise per litre in the second hike in a week—driven by West Asia supply shocks, which is pushing inflation concerns and potentially constraining emerging market growth. Higher commodity costs globally can weigh on US corporate margins and shift Fed rate expectations. The US has extended its sanctions waiver on Russian oil through June 17, keeping crude supply flows relatively stable but leaving geopolitical uncertainty as a persistent tail risk for energy prices and inflation data that the Fed watches closely.

Alright, let’s start with something really intuitive. Imagine you’re trying to decide whether today is a good day to sell lemonade. You want to pick a day when people are thirsty and willing to pay premium prices. So you look at the temperature outside. Today it’s 85 degrees. That sounds hot, right? But here’s the thing—if you live in Phoenix, 85 degrees in May is actually pretty cool. If you live in Minnesota, 85 degrees is scorching. The same number means completely different things depending on the context of what’s normal for that place. So you need to know: is 85 degrees hot compared to what people around here usually experience? That’s the real question that tells you whether you can charge premium prices. IV Rank is like looking at today’s temperature in isolation. IV Percentile is like knowing what the normal temperature range has been for the past year, so you can actually judge whether 85 degrees is unusual and valuable.

Now let’s connect this to what’s actually happening in SPY options right now. IV Rank and IV Percentile sound like they’re measuring the same thing, but they’re measuring it against different baselines. IV Rank compares today’s implied volatility—which is sitting at 17.18 right now on SPY—to the highest and lowest IV we’ve seen over the past 252 trading days. So if IV has ranged from 10 to 30 over the year, and today it’s at 17.18, IV Rank tells you where in that range you sit. It’s a percentile of the high-low range. But here’s where it gets tricky. IV Percentile doesn’t care about the absolute high or low. Instead, it answers a different question: out of all the days in the past year, how many days had lower IV than today? Today, SPY’s IV Percentile is 32.95. That means IV has been lower than today’s 17.18 on about 33 percent of the trading days in the past year, and higher on about 67 percent of them. Notice something interesting? IV Rank is 88.55—that sounds really high, like we’re near the top of the range. But IV Percentile is only 32.95—that says we’re actually in the lower third of what’s normal. These two numbers are telling you almost opposite stories, and that matters enormously when you’re thinking about selling premium.

The natural question here is: how can both numbers be true at the same time? Here’s how to think about it. IV Rank is measuring where we sit in the recent extreme range. Maybe IV spiked to 30 two months ago during some market panic, and the floor was 10 back in January when everything was calm. If IV is at 17.18 now, that’s pretty far up from 10, so IV Rank says 88.55—we’re near the top of that range. But IV Percentile is asking a different question entirely. It’s counting how many individual days had lower IV than today. If the market has been volatile for most of the year, with IV spending a lot of time at 18, 19, 20, then 17.18 is actually below average. Even though 17.18 is high compared to the extreme low point, it’s low compared to what’s been typical. So IV Percentile would be low. This is why IV Percentile is actually more reliable for traders like you who are thinking about selling premium. You don’t care where IV sits relative to the absolute high from six months ago. You care whether IV is elevated compared to what’s normal right now, because that’s what tells you whether premium is actually rich or cheap.

Let’s get specific about why this matters for your premium-selling strategy today. SPY is at 733.82, and we’re looking at the June 18 expiration, which is 30 days out. The ATM calls at the 734 strike are trading at 17.18 IV. Now, if you were only looking at IV Rank at 88.55, you’d think, “Wow, IV is super elevated, this is a great time to sell premium.” And sometimes that’s true. But IV Percentile at 32.95 is telling you something more nuanced. It’s saying that even though IV is in the upper part of its recent range, it’s actually been lower than this on about two-thirds of trading days this year. So the question becomes: why is IV Rank so high if IV Percentile is so low? The answer is that the range itself has compressed. The absolute high from the past year might be 30, and the absolute low might be 10, so 17.18 looks high on that scale. But the market has actually been spending most of its time between 16 and 20. In that context, 17.18 is pretty normal, maybe even a touch on the low side.

Here’s where this changes your actual trading decision. If you’re considering a call spread to sell premium—say, selling the 738 call and buying the 740 call for June expiration—you need to know whether you’re being compensated for the risk. IV Percentile tells you that compensation is modest. You’re not in a volatility spike where premium is juicy. You’re in a normalized market where IV is doing what it usually does. That means your edge on a defined-risk spread isn’t as fat as IV Rank might have led you to believe. The Greeks will reflect this too. Notice that the ATM call has a delta of 0.53, which is right where it should be for an ATM strike. But vega—the sensitivity to IV changes—is going to be meaningful but not explosive. If IV drops from 17.18 to 16, that’s a move that happens on about a third of the trading days in a normal year, and it’ll hurt your short call position. IV Percentile is essentially saying: don’t expect IV to stay elevated, because it’s already at a level where it’s below average. The market isn’t panicked. There’s no fear premium baked in. You’re selling premium in a relatively calm environment.

This is the systematic trader’s insight: IV Rank and IV Percentile often diverge precisely when you need to know the difference most. IV Percentile gives you the real story about whether premium is actually rich or just looks rich on the chart. In today’s SPY market, with IV Percentile at 32.95, you’re being told that if you sell premium, you’re selling at a price that’s below the typical price for this year. That’s not a great setup for a premium seller. You’d rather be selling when IV Percentile is above 70 or 80—that’s when you’re actually compensated for taking on the risk that IV will collapse. Right now, the market is pricing in relatively normal volatility. Healthcare is leading with a 0.88% gain, while Materials is lagging with a -2.07% drop, but that sector rotation isn’t showing up as elevated fear in the overall IV structure. The lesson here is simple: always check IV Percentile before you commit to a premium-selling strategy. It’s the number that actually tells you whether you’re being paid to take risk or just accepting normal market conditions.

SPY closed down 0.65 percent today at 733.82, a modest pullback that masks some real divergence happening under the hood. We’re down about ten points from last Wednesday’s close of 742.31, so we’re in the middle of a consolidation week after that nice pop on May 14th when we hit 748. The move today isn’t dramatic in isolation, but it’s the third down day in the last four sessions, and that’s worth paying attention to. Volume came in at 6.5 million shares, which is reasonable but not exceptional, so this isn’t panic selling. It’s more like a slow leak of conviction, and that’s actually important context for anyone thinking about selling premium into this.

Let’s talk about what actually drove the decline, because the sector breakdown tells a much clearer story than the headline number. Materials got absolutely hammered, down 2.07 percent, which is the biggest drag on the index by a wide margin. Consumer Discretionary fell 1.25 percent, Industrials dropped 1.22 percent, and Technology — which you’d think would be leading given all the AI narrative — actually declined 1.17 percent. Those three sectors combined are responsible for most of SPY’s weakness today. On the flip side, Healthcare was the only real leader, up 0.88 percent, with Consumer Staples adding 0.38 percent. Energy barely moved at plus 0.16 percent, and Real Estate and Utilities were essentially flat. So what we’re seeing is a classic risk-off rotation: cyclicals and growth getting sold, defensives getting bought. That’s the real story.

The Materials decline is particularly interesting because it usually signals either a slowdown in growth expectations or weakness in commodity prices. We’ve got CPI that came in hotter than expected at 3.95 percent year-over-year, up 63 basis points from the prior reading, and that’s probably spooking some of the reflation trades that had been working. The ten-year Treasury is up 12 basis points to 4.59 percent, and the two-year is up 9 basis points to 4.09 percent, so the curve is steepening a bit. That’s usually a sign that the market is pricing in either more inflation or a slower Fed cut cycle, and that’s not great for materials and cyclicals. Technology’s weakness could also be tied to the higher rates — growth stocks don’t like rising yields — but honestly, there’s also been some noise around AI valuations and whether the earnings will actually justify the multiples we’ve paid. Berkshire’s recent moves, which we saw in the headlines this morning, show them rotating into dividend-paying stocks and away from some of the mega-cap tech names, and that’s probably influencing sentiment.

Now let’s talk about the IV picture, because this is where it gets interesting for you as a premium seller. IV rank is sitting at 88.55, which is extremely elevated. That means we’re in the 88th percentile of IV readings over the past year. The current IV itself is 17.18, and the VIX closed at 17.99. Here’s the thing: IV rank that high tells you that implied volatility has been compressed for a while, and we’re now seeing it expand. That’s actually a gift for someone who wants to sell premium, because you’re getting paid more for the same risk. The IV percentile is only 32.95, which means we’re not at the absolute top of the range, but we’re definitely in the upper half. The skew is also interesting — calls are trading at 17.18 IV at the money, but puts are trading at 14.56 IV at the same strike. That’s a 260 basis point spread, which tells you the market is still pricing in more downside risk than upside risk. That’s a classic bear market skew, and it’s a signal that selling call spreads might be more attractive than selling put spreads right now.

For price levels, we need to watch 730 as immediate support — that’s where we bounced off on May 15th during that 1.2 percent down day. Below that, 725 is the next level of interest. On the upside, 738 to 740 is where we’ve been consolidating, and if we can clear 742, we’re back to testing the 748 high from last week. The 52-week range is 575.60 to 749.53, so we’re still in the upper half of that range, but we’ve given back about 15 points from the recent high. That’s not catastrophic, but it’s enough to make people nervous about whether the rally has legs.

On the calendar, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in two days on May 21st, which is medium importance. That’s not going to move the market dramatically, but it’s worth noting. The real event is the Fed Meeting on June 10th, which is 22 days away. That’s outside our immediate window, but it’s definitely on everyone’s radar. With CPI running hot at 3.95 percent and the ten-year yield rising, the market is probably pricing in a higher-for-longer scenario, and that’s going to weigh on growth stocks. There are no major earnings releases in the next seven days, so we don’t have that volatility catalyst to worry about.

Here’s the practical takeaway for your position this week: IV rank at 88.55 combined with a 30-day call IV of 17.18 and put IV of 14.56 means you’re getting paid premium for selling calls, but the skew suggests the market is genuinely worried about downside. If you’re thinking about a defined-risk call spread — say, selling the 740 call and buying the 745 call — you’re collecting decent premium with that elevated IV, and you’ve got a 5-point cushion before you’re at max loss. The risk-reward is asymmetric in your favor right now, especially if you believe the index consolidates between 730 and 745 over the next month.

Here’s the thing that doesn’t get talked about enough in senior PM circles: there’s a real cost to the “I stay strategic, engineers stay tactical” model, and it’s not what you think. Teresa Torres went from occasional tinkering to spending sixty percent of her time actually building AI tools for discovery. And the insight isn’t that PMs should all learn to code. It’s that when you’re not in the weeds of how something actually gets made, you’re making discovery decisions blind.

What that means in practice is you’re optimizing for the wrong constraints. You think you’re being efficient by staying at the thirty-thousand-foot level, but you’re actually creating a game of telephone between what’s possible and what you’re asking for. When Torres started building her own discovery tools, she immediately saw where the friction was—not in the concept, but in the data plumbing, the real-time sync, the edge cases that would never come up in a requirements doc.

Think about it like managing a defined-risk spread. You can read about theta decay and IV crush all day, but until you’re actually managing positions into expiration, watching how your Greeks shift when the market moves, you don’t have intuition. You have theory. Torres is saying the same thing about product discovery—you can’t delegate away the learning. The move here is not to become an engineer, but to build something small yourself, even if it’s a script or a no-code tool that solves one actual problem you have. Not as a side project. As part of how you do discovery.

Why this matters for you specifically: you’re building intuition for systematic trading. That requires you to live inside the mechanics, not just understand them conceptually. Same thing applies to product. The PMs who move into GPM roles are the ones who can see three moves ahead because they’ve actually felt where the resistance is. They’ve built something, shipped something, watched it break in production. That’s not technical debt on your PM responsibilities—that’s the only way you actually learn what’s possible.

Here’s your move this week: pick one small discovery problem you have—maybe it’s understanding how your users actually navigate a flow, or what questions they ask in a specific moment. Build a quick Claude Code agent or a simple script that helps you answer it yourself. Spend two hours max. The goal isn’t the output. It’s to feel the gap between what you think is hard and what actually is.

What we covered

  • World news: Trump-Xi Deal Signals Tariff Pause, Berkshire Reshuffles, Japan Yields Spike
  • SPY learning: IV Rank: Reading the Room Before You Trade
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Consumer Staples Lead as Tech Stumbles; IV Rank at Extremes
  • PM/analytics: No viable selection

Summary

Americas: Berkshire Hathaway just dropped $2.6 billion into Delta Airlines and cranked up its Alphabet position by 224%, signaling Buffett’s conviction in big-cap tech and cyclical recovery. This matters because it’s one of the sharpest portfolio rotations from Berkshire in years—when the Oracle moves this decisively, institutional capital tends to follow. Europe: Nothing with material trade or central bank impact today. Asia/China: Trump came out of his Xi summit talking up soybean and rare earth deals while both sides claim tariff cuts are coming, but the details are already diverging between Washington and Beijing. Taiwan also got a curveball when Trump said he’s not looking for independence there—that’s geopolitical risk shifting in real time for semiconductor supply chains. India: The RBI announced it’s not activating the countercyclical capital buffer, meaning they’re holding steady on policy tightness and not signaling imminent rate cuts. This keeps India’s capital flows sensitive to any Fed pivot. Middle East & Africa: Iran is attempting to reopen its stock market after a lengthy war-driven closure, a symbolic move toward normalizing capital flows, though the geopolitical tension with the US remains live given Trump’s recent ‘clock is ticking’ warning. Asia Pacific: Japan’s long-term bond yields just hit record highs as fiscal concerns mount, and the Nikkei is overhauling its 225 index rules—both signals of structural stress in one of the world’s largest equity markets and potential capital reallocation away from JGBs.

Let me start with something you’ve probably experienced. Imagine you’re at a movie theater on a random Tuesday afternoon versus opening night of a blockbuster. On Tuesday, tickets are cheap. Nobody’s really here. The theater is half empty. But on opening night? Same theater, same movie, same seats. Now there’s a line around the block. Tickets cost way more. The energy is completely different. The demand has shifted dramatically. Now here’s the thing—the movie hasn’t changed. The seats are identical. What changed is how much people want to be there right now, and that urgency gets priced in. That’s the intuition you need to understand before we talk about options.

Now let’s translate that to what’s actually happening in the market. IV—implied volatility—is the market’s expectation of how much SPY is going to move around. But here’s where most people get confused: IV itself changes. It goes up and down. So the question becomes: is the current IV high or low compared to what we’ve seen historically? That’s what IV Rank measures. It tells you where today’s implied volatility sits within the range of the last 52 weeks. It’s a percentile. A reading of zero means IV is at its lowest point in the past year. A reading of 100 means IV is at its highest point in the past year. Right now, on May 18th, SPY’s IV Rank is sitting at 100. That’s the opening night blockbuster scenario. The market is pricing in maximum expected movement. We’re at the absolute top of the volatility range we’ve seen over the past 12 months.

The natural question here is: how do I know if 100 is actually extreme? Here’s how to think about it. IV Rank gives you context. SPY’s current implied volatility is 16.41, which sounds like a number, but on its own it’s meaningless. Is that high? Is that low? You can’t tell without the frame of reference. But when you know that 16.41 puts us at the 100th percentile—meaning we’ve only seen IV this high or higher zero percent of the time in the past year—suddenly you have information. The market is in a state of maximum fear or uncertainty right now. Compare this to the 52-week range: SPY has traded from a low of 575 to a high of 749. We’re at 739.91 today. The market has moved hard, and that movement has driven volatility up to its extreme.

Let’s connect this to actual trading. You’re considering a premium-selling strategy on SPY. Maybe you’re thinking about a call spread or a put spread. Here’s what IV Rank of 100 actually means for your decision-making. When IV is at the extreme high end, option premiums are fat. They’re expensive. That sounds good if you’re selling—you get paid more. But there’s a catch, and this is critical: when IV is at 100, it’s usually there for a reason. The market is uncertain. There’s fear. There’s volatility. And here’s the psychological trap: traders see that fat premium and think, “Perfect, I’ll sell this expensive option.” But what they’re not thinking about is that if IV contracts—if that blockbuster movie night energy dies down and people stop showing up—those options you sold become less valuable faster. You’re fighting the wind. IV tends to mean-revert. When it’s at 100, it doesn’t stay there. It comes down. And when it does, you win on the IV contraction, sure. But the market is also telling you something: it expects movement. If you sell a spread at IV Rank 100 and SPY actually moves in the direction you’re short, you’re getting punished on both fronts—direction and IV expansion working against you.

Here’s the real implication for how you should think about this specific moment. With IV Rank at 100 and 25 days until the June 12 expiration, you’re looking at an interesting asymmetry. The 740 call is trading at 16.41 IV. The 740 put is trading at 13.94 IV. Notice that? Calls are more expensive than puts. That’s skew, and it exists because the market has just moved hard and traders are nervous about more upside. If you’re thinking about selling premium, this environment is saying: yes, you can collect fat premiums right now. But you’re getting paid for taking on the risk that the market stays volatile. The natural question here is: should I be selling or buying when IV Rank is at 100? Here’s how to think about it. Selling premium at IV Rank 100 is like selling insurance right after a hurricane. You get paid a lot because everyone is scared. But you’re exposed if another hurricane comes. Buying premium at IV Rank 100 is like buying insurance at peak price. You’re overpaying for protection. Neither is inherently wrong, but your edge depends on your view. If you think the market is going to calm down—if you believe IV will contract from 100 toward the 40 or 50 percentile range—then selling premium now makes sense. You’re selling at the peak. But if you think the volatility is justified, that we’re heading into another leg of uncertainty, then you’re selling at exactly the wrong time. You’re fighting the direction of mean reversion. Right now, with the market up 0.1 percent today but down hard from the 749 high just days ago, with technology lagging and consumer staples leading, the IV Rank is screaming that the market is uncertain about direction. That’s your context. That’s what you’re pricing in when you look at those call and put IVs. You’re not just looking at Greeks anymore. You’re reading the room.

Alright, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 739.91, up just a tenth of a percent on the day. Now, that sounds sleepy on the surface, but context matters here. We’re coming off a brutal Friday last week where the index dropped 1.2 percent, and before that we had a two-day rip higher that took us from 738 to 748. So today’s flatness is actually a pause in what’s been a volatile week. We’re still down about 8 bucks from the 748 high we hit on Thursday, which tells you the market is still digesting something. Volume came in at 6.06 million shares, which is below average, so we’re not seeing conviction in either direction right now.

Let’s talk about what actually moved the needle today, because the sector breakdown is where the real story lives. Consumer Staples, the XLP, led the charge with a 1.18 percent gain. That’s your defensive play—the stuff people buy no matter what. Financials followed with 1.16 percent, and Real Estate added 1.1 percent. These are all lower-volatility, income-oriented sectors, which tells you the market was in a risk-off mood despite the tiny positive close. Now flip to the laggards. Technology, the XLK, fell 0.83 percent. That’s the biggest drag on the index. Energy dropped 0.45 percent, and Industrials slipped 0.44 percent. So we had defensive sectors carrying the day while the growth and cyclical stuff got sold. That’s a classic risk-off rotation, even if SPY itself barely moved.

Why is this happening? Well, look at the macro backdrop. CPI year-over-year just came in at 3.95 percent, up 63 basis points from the previous reading of 3.32 percent. That’s a meaningful tick higher, and it’s keeping the Fed in a holding pattern at 3.64 percent. The 10-year Treasury is sitting at 4.47 percent, up a single basis point, but the 2-year is at 4.0 percent, up 2 basis points. The yield curve has steepened slightly to 50 basis points, which is positive, but the inflation print is real and it’s not going away. Consumer sentiment also took a hit, dropping 3.3 points to 53.3. So you’ve got inflation concerns, softer sentiment, and the market is rotating into safety. Tech gets hit because higher rates and inflation concerns compress multiples on growth stocks. That’s the mechanical story.

Now let’s talk about what this means for you as someone thinking about selling premium. IV rank is sitting at 100.0, which means we are at the absolute top of the range for implied volatility over the past year. Current IV is 16.41, and the VIX closed at 18.77. That’s elevated but not panic territory. Here’s the thing: IV rank at 100 is a screaming sell signal for premium sellers. You are getting paid maximum dollars for taking directional risk. The problem is, you’re also getting paid maximum dollars because the market is uncertain. That inflation print, the Fed’s next move, the China trade situation—there’s real uncertainty priced in. So yes, you want to be a seller here, but you need to be disciplined about strike selection and position sizing. This is not the time to get greedy and sell naked calls at 745. The premium is fat, but the risk is real.

Let’s establish the key levels you need to watch. Immediate resistance is 741.40, which is today’s high. If we break above that, the next target is 745, which is a psychological level and sits between today’s low and Thursday’s high. Support is sitting at 737.56, today’s low, and below that you’ve got 735 as a technical level. The 52-week low is way down at 575.60, but that’s not relevant for this week’s trading. The 52-week high is 749.53, so we’re about 10 points below that. For your spreads, you’re looking at a range-bound market right now, which actually favors defined-risk structures. The ATM strike at 740 has a delta of 0.53 on the call side and negative 0.46 on the put side, so you’re right in the middle of the action.

Looking ahead at the calendar, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in three days on May 21st. That’s medium importance, but mortgage rates have been sticky, and any surprise there could move the market. More importantly, the Fed meeting is 23 days away on June 10th. That’s outside your immediate trading window if you’re thinking about June expiration, but it’s absolutely on the radar for anyone holding into mid-June. There are no major earnings in the next seven days, so you’re not dealing with binary events. That’s actually good news for premium sellers because it means the move is likely to be driven by macro data and sentiment, not surprise earnings beats or misses.

Here’s your practical takeaway for this week: IV rank at 100 is a gift for selling premium, but the inflation data and risk-off rotation tell you the market is nervous. If you’re building a short premium position, consider selling call spreads above 742 or put spreads below 735 rather than naked positions. The premium is fat enough to make the trade work even with tighter spreads, and you’re protecting yourself against the tail risk that’s clearly priced into the VIX.

I need to be straight with you: none of these posts are actionable for your actual use case right now.

You’re a senior PM learning to think systematically about defined-risk spreads on SPY. You’re building intuition around Greeks, IV rank, position sizing, and trade mechanics. You’re trying to think like a quant, not a PM.

The input here is pure PM content—Claude Code workflows, AI hardware supply chains, hiring practices. Smart stuff, sure. But it has zero signal for what you’re actually working on this week: maybe analyzing why your short call deltas are drifting faster than expected, or figuring out the right IV percentile threshold before you scale into iron condors.

Here’s what I’d tell you instead: if you’re serious about becoming a systematic trader and staying sharp as a PM, you need to separate those reading streams completely. Don’t try to force-fit product strategy frameworks onto position management. They use different decision-making muscles.

For the podcast segment this week, I’d recommend we either pivot the input source to something with real trading signal—maybe a post on portfolio construction, position correlation, or how professional traders think about drawdown—or we acknowledge that today’s PM content doesn’t have a home in your daily trading prep.

The move here is honesty: a senior PM knows the difference between interesting and relevant. These posts are interesting. They’re not relevant to what you’re trying to build this week.

Summary

This is notable because Ackman doesn’t move quietly—when he builds stakes, he’s signaling conviction on mega-cap tech fundamentals at a time when the market’s been jittery on valuations. The Nvidia H200 chip export question is huge because it directly affects whether US semiconductor companies can access the Chinese market, which flows straight through to earnings and valuations for big tech. India: Government refiners hiked diesel and petrol prices by 3 rupees per liter as West Asia tensions keep crude elevated, with more spikes likely on the horizon. India’s energy costs feed into inflation expectations, which matters for emerging market capital flows and the dollar strength that affects SPY. Let me start with something you’ve probably experienced without thinking about it much. Imagine you’re trying to sell your car, and you list it for five thousand dollars. On day one, you’ve got three interested buyers lined up. Fast forward two weeks — you’ve got zero calls, and now you’re thinking maybe I should drop it to forty-eight hundred. What happened? Time passed. The longer your car sits on the lot, the less appealing it becomes, and the less money you can ask for it. Nobody’s paying full price for a car that’s been gathering dust. That’s the intuition we’re after right now. The value of something — in this case your car — decays simply because time is moving forward. It’s not because the car got worse. It’s not because the market crashed. It’s literally just that another day went by, and that makes the thing worth less.

Here’s another angle. You’re at a movie theater, and they’re selling tickets for a showing that starts in two hours. Right now, a ticket costs fifteen bucks. But what if you come back tomorrow and the movie’s starting in one hour? That ticket might still cost fifteen. But what if you come back the day the movie actually plays, and there’s only thirty minutes until showtime? Now that ticket is worth more to the theater because scarcity just went up — there’s almost no time left to sell it. But here’s the twist: if you’re the one who bought that ticket a month ago, and you’re trying to resell it, the opposite happens. A month ago, your ticket was valuable because you had time to find a buyer. Today, with thirty minutes left, your resale value just tanked. Nobody wants a ticket that’s about to expire. The closer you get to that showtime, the faster your ticket loses value — not because the movie got worse, but because the clock is running out.

Now let’s translate this into what actually happens in the options world, and I’m going to use real numbers from today’s market. SPY is trading at 739.55 right now, and we’re looking at options that expire on June 12th — that’s 28 days from now. When you buy or sell an option, you’re buying or selling time, essentially. That time has value, and it’s called theta. Here’s the thing: theta is the daily erosion of an option’s price due to time passing, assuming nothing else changes. The stock could stay flat. The market could be completely calm. But every single day that goes by, that option loses a tiny bit of value just from the calendar flipping. It’s like interest on a savings account, except it works in reverse — instead of earning something, you’re losing something every day.

The natural question here is: who benefits from this time decay? Here’s how to think about it. If you’re the person who sold an option, time decay is working for you. Every day that passes, the option you sold becomes worth slightly less, which means if you wanted to buy it back and lock in your profit, you could do it cheaper. If you’re the person who bought an option, time decay is working against you. You paid for that option on day one, and on day two, without the stock moving at all, it’s worth a tiny bit less. Over 28 days until expiration, that erosion adds up. Think of it like this: when you sell your car for five thousand dollars, you want to sell it fast because every day it sits there, it’s worth less. But when you bought that ticket a month ago, you want the show to happen soon because every day that passes, your resale value drops. The seller of the option is like the car lot owner — they benefit from time. The buyer of the option is like the person who bought the movie ticket early — they get hurt by time.

Let’s make this concrete with what’s happening today. The 740 call option — right at the money where SPY is trading — has about 28 days until it expires. That call is currently worth something. Let’s say it’s worth about three dollars and fifty cents, just to pick a number. Tomorrow, if SPY doesn’t move at all, if the market is totally flat, that call will be worth slightly less than three-fifty. Maybe three-forty-eight. That two-cent loss? That’s theta at work. It doesn’t sound like much, but multiply that by 28 days, and you’re talking about real money. Here’s the key insight: theta accelerates as you get closer to expiration. In the first week, you might lose a penny a day. In the final week, you might lose ten cents a day. The time decay gets faster and faster as the deadline approaches, just like how that movie ticket loses value way faster on the day of the show than it does a month before.

So what does this actually mean for you as a trader right now, on May 15th, 2026? Here’s the trading implication. SPY is at 739.55, and volatility — that’s the IV we talk about — is at 17 percent. That’s actually pretty high historically, which means options are expensive right now. If you’re thinking about buying options, you’re buying something that’s already pricey, and you’re immediately starting to lose value to theta every single day. You need the stock to move significantly in your direction just to break even, because you’ve got time decay working against you. Let me walk through the reasoning. The 740 call that’s right at the money has a delta of 0.53, which means it’s roughly a coin flip whether it finishes in the money or out. But more importantly, every single day that passes between now and June 12th, you’re losing money to theta, even if SPY stays exactly where it is. If you bought this call, you’d need SPY to move up enough to compensate for that daily bleed. That’s why buying options is hard — you’re fighting the clock.

Now flip the scenario. If you’re thinking about selling options — maybe selling a put or a call — theta is your friend. You’re collecting money upfront from someone who’s paying for that time value, and every day that passes, that premium you collected becomes more and more likely to be yours to keep. The 740 put, which is also right at the money with a delta of negative 0.47, is losing value every single day. If you sold that put, you’re on the winning side of that decay. The buyer paid you a premium, and that premium is shrinking by the day. In 28 days, when this option expires, if SPY is anywhere near 740, your short put is worthless, and you keep the entire premium. That’s the power of theta for sellers. Given that IV rank is at 100 today — meaning volatility is at the extreme high end of its historical range — options are expensive, which means theta is particularly valuable right now. If you’re a seller, you’re getting paid a lot for that time value. If you’re a buyer, you’re paying a lot for something that’s decaying against you every day. That’s the real decision point: in a high IV environment like today, selling premium and letting theta work for you is a much more favorable trade than buying premium and fighting theta.

Alright, so SPY had a rough day today. We closed at 739.55, down 1.15 percent, and I want to be clear about what this means in context because it matters for how you’re thinking about your positions this week. The bigger picture is that we’re still up about 2.3 percent from where we started the week, so this is more of a consolidation day than a reversal.

Now let’s talk about what actually drove this move, because the sector breakdown tells you everything. Technology absolutely got hammered today — XLK fell 2.27 percent and was the single biggest drag on the index. That’s a meaningful move in a broad-based selloff. Materials followed close behind, down 2.15 percent, and Industrials dropped 1.56 percent. So what you’re seeing here is a classic risk-off rotation — money coming out of the growth and cyclical names and rotating into the defensive, commodity-linked stuff. That’s the opposite of what we saw yesterday, which tells me there’s some genuine uncertainty creeping back in.

The question is why. And honestly, the headlines give us some clues. You’ve got the Trump-Xi summit wrapping up in Beijing with talks dominated by trade, oil, and Taiwan — that’s geopolitical noise that typically makes growth investors nervous. You’ve also got CPI data that’s been creeping higher. The year-over-year number came in at 3.95 percent, up 63 basis points from the previous reading. That’s not catastrophic, but it’s moving in the wrong direction when the Fed is sitting at 3.64 percent on the funds rate. Meanwhile, Energy benefits from any geopolitical tension and from the possibility that inflation stays sticky. That’s the macro story driving today’s sector rotation.

Let’s talk about volatility because this is crucial for your options decisions. IV Rank is sitting at 100 — that’s the absolute top of the range. We’re at the 27th percentile for IV Percentile, which means we’re elevated but not at historical extremes. The VIX closed at 18.64, up from 17.87 yesterday. What this tells me is that the market is pricing in more uncertainty than it was, but we’re not in panic mode. Current IV is at 17.0, which is still relatively benign. Here’s the practical takeaway: if you’re thinking about selling premium — whether that’s short calls or short puts — you’re getting paid a little better than you were yesterday, but you’re not getting paid spectacularly. The IV Rank at 100 means you’re at the high end of the range for this stock, so it’s not the worst time to be a seller, but it’s also not screaming opportunity. If you’re a buyer of premium, you’re paying up a bit, but the absolute levels are still reasonable for a 28-day expiration.

Let me give you the key price levels to watch because these matter for your strike selection. We’ve got immediate support at 737.96 — that’s today’s low and it’s a hard floor we tested this morning. Below that, the next meaningful level is around 735, which would represent about a 0.6 percent move lower. On the upside, we need to reclaim 741.79, which was today’s open. If we can get back through that, the next resistance is 748.17, which is where we closed yesterday. That’s only about 1.2 percent above current levels, so it’s not a huge move. The 52-week high is 749.53, so we’re still well within the range we’ve established over the past few months. For your options trading, the ATM strike at 740 is right where the action is — we’ve got 2,130 open interest on the calls and 1,516 on the puts, so there’s decent liquidity there.

Looking ahead at the calendar, we don’t have a ton of major economic data coming in the next 48 hours, which is actually a bit of a relief after the volatility we’ve seen. The Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update is coming in six days, so that’s not immediately relevant. The big event on the horizon is the Fed Meeting on June 10th, which is 26 days away. That’s outside our immediate window, but it’s definitely in the back of everyone’s mind given the inflation concerns we just talked about. There are no major earnings releases in the next week, so we’re in a relatively quiet period from a calendar perspective. That means any moves we see are going to be driven by sentiment and macro positioning rather than company-specific catalysts. That’s actually useful information for you because it means the broad market direction matters more than individual stock picks right now.

Here’s my one-sentence takeaway for you as you’re thinking about your position this week: the IV Rank at 100 combined with the sector rotation away from tech suggests this is a decent spot to sell premium on a bounce back toward 741-742, but keep your stops tight because the geopolitical backdrop and inflation concerns could push us lower if sentiment deteriorates further.

Here’s the thing that separates senior PMs from the rest: most people treat prototyping as a delivery mechanism. You build a prototype, you ship it, you learn. But what if the prototype itself becomes your thinking tool? That’s the actual unlock that AI-powered prototyping gives you, and it changes how you validate assumptions before you ever commit engineering resources.

What that means in practice is this. You’re sitting in a discovery meeting. Someone proposes a feature. Instead of running a Slack poll or setting up a research session that takes three weeks, you spin up a quick interactive prototype in an afternoon. Not a pixel-perfect mockup — something clickable, something that lets you and your team actually experience the interaction. You’re not validating market demand yet. You’re validating whether the core interaction even makes sense.

I’ll give you a concrete example. Say you’re deciding between two onboarding flows — one that front-loads all the setup, another that spreads it across the first week. A traditional approach: you pick one, ship it, measure retention curves for two weeks, then iterate. The AI-powered approach: you prototype both in a day. You and your stakeholders actually click through them. You catch that the second flow has a confusing handoff point that nobody would have noticed in a spec. You iterate the prototype. Now when you brief engineering, you’re not asking them to build a hunch — you’re asking them to build something you’ve already validated with real interaction.

The move here is to stop thinking of prototyping as the final step before handoff. Make it the first step after the idea lands. This is especially relevant for you as you’re learning options trading, actually — same principle. You don’t trade a new strategy with real capital. You paper-trade it first. You’re validating the logic before you commit resources. Prototyping is your paper-trading for product.

Here’s what to do this week: pick one feature your team is debating right now. The one where people have different mental models of how it should work. Spend two hours building a throwaway prototype — use Claude, Figma, whatever gets you to interactive fastest. Don’t make it pretty. Just make it clickable. Show it to three people on your team separately and watch where they get confused. That’s your actual validation data, and you got it before a single line of production code landed.

Summary

Trump wrapped up his Beijing summit with Xi Jinping, with both sides claiming wins but sending mixed signals—the US focused on trade and tariffs while China emphasized Taiwan stability and partnership language. Traders are betting on a tariff truce extension and Boeing aircraft purchases, though the lack of Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang on the delegation signals continued chip export restrictions remain non-negotiable. A resilient UK economy reduces recession fears in a major US trading partner and keeps sterling from collapsing, which has spillover effects on dollar strength and commodity pricing. No major market-relevant stories emerged from India today. These are long-cycle structural plays rather than near-term market movers, though they underscore Japan’s pivot toward AI and the continued strength of Japanese consumer brands in the US.

Alright, let me paint you a picture. Imagine you’re a parking lot attendant, and you’ve decided to sell reserved parking spots to people who might show up tomorrow. You sell fifty spots for ten bucks each, thinking most people won’t actually come. The closer you get to tomorrow, the more certain you become about whether people are actually coming or not. But here’s the thing: if the weather suddenly changes and a huge event gets announced, suddenly everyone wants a spot. Now you’re scrambling to find fifty parking spaces you promised away, and the closer it gets to tomorrow, the more frantic and expensive that scramble becomes. That scramble — that forced, accelerating adjustment — that’s gamma risk. It’s the cost of being wrong when there’s almost no time left to be right.

Now let’s translate that to what’s actually happening in the SPY options market right now. We’re looking at June 12th expiration, which is twenty-nine days away. SPY is sitting at 745.14, and we’ve got an IV rank of 100 — meaning volatility is at the absolute top of its one-year range. That’s important context. When you sell an option, you’re essentially taking the other side of that bet. You collect the premium upfront, which feels great. But gamma is the rate at which your delta changes. Delta tells you how much your position moves with the stock. Gamma tells you how fast that delta accelerates. The natural question here is: why does this matter if I’m just collecting premium? Here’s how to think about it: when you sell a call option, you’re short delta — meaning you make money if the stock goes down. But as the stock moves against you, your delta gets worse faster and faster. On a thirty-day option, gamma is manageable. On a three-day option, gamma is a sniper. With twenty-nine days to go, gamma is still a threat, but it’s not yet a crisis. But watch what happens in the next three weeks.

Let’s say you sold the 745 call — the at-the-money strike right where SPY is trading now. Your delta is 0.54, meaning you’re effectively short about fifty-four shares per contract. Gamma is low right now because there’s time. But here’s the mechanism: every dollar SPY moves up, your gamma accelerates your delta loss. If SPY jumps to 750 in the next week, your delta might not just go to 0.59 — it could jump to 0.65 or 0.70, depending on gamma. And the closer you get to expiration, the more violent that acceleration becomes. On the day before expiration, if you’re short that 745 call and SPY is at 750, you’re essentially short seventy-five shares of a stock that just moved against you five dollars. You’d be down 375 bucks on that contract alone. But worse — you can’t hedge it gradually. The gamma forces your hand. You have to buy shares to protect yourself, and you’re buying them at the worst possible time because everyone else is doing the same thing.

So here’s what this means for you as a trader right now, today, with SPY at 745.14 and IV rank at 100. The fact that IV rank is maxed out tells us volatility has been historically low and just spiked. That’s actually a dangerous time to be a short gamma seller because mean reversion often brings big moves. If you’re tempted to sell premium right now because the IV is juicy, you need to understand you’re not just selling volatility — you’re also taking on the risk that volatility stays elevated and creates the exact kind of moves that destroy short gamma positions. The natural question here is: so should I just never sell options? Not quite. Here’s how to think about it: selling options makes sense when you have a thesis about direction or when you’re willing to actively manage the position. But selling options passively near expiration when IV is at the top of its range is like leaving that parking lot attendant job during a thunderstorm and expecting no one to show up. The market is telling you through that 100 IV rank that uncertainty is elevated. Gamma is the tax you pay for being wrong while time is running out. With twenty-nine days to expiration, you have time to adjust, to roll, to hedge. With three days to expiration, you’re paying that tax in real time, and the bill comes due fast.

The practical move here is this: if you’re thinking about selling SPY calls or puts into this IV rank of 100, you want to either sell further out in time — go to July or August expiration instead of June — or you want to sell wider spreads so your gamma risk is capped. Selling the 745 call naked means unlimited gamma risk on the upside. Selling a 745-750 call spread means your gamma risk is capped at five dollars. That’s the difference between a parking attendant scrambling to find fifty spots and one who’s only responsible for five. Today’s market is showing you that financials are leading, up 0.65 percent, and the overall market moved up 0.38 percent. That’s a calm day. But with IV rank at 100, the market is pricing in the possibility that calm doesn’t last. Gamma is the cost of being short when that assumption breaks. Understand that cost, and you’ll understand why experienced traders respect time decay but fear gamma risk.

Alright, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 745.14, up 0.38 percent on the day. Now, that doesn’t sound like a massive move, but here’s the context that matters: we’re up about seven and a half points from last Friday’s close of 737.62. So over the past five trading days, we’ve had a pretty consistent grind higher. Yesterday was the big day with a 0.56 percent jump, and today we added another 0.38 percent. This is the kind of steady accumulation that doesn’t grab headlines but definitely matters for your portfolio positioning. Volume came in at 5.5 million shares, which is moderate — nothing extreme, but enough to suggest there’s real conviction behind this move.

Now let’s talk about what actually drove this. Financials led the charge today, with XLF up 0.65 percent. That’s your biggest gainer and it’s telling us something important about the macro backdrop. Technology followed closely behind at 0.54 percent, and Communication Services at 0.52 percent. So the big-cap growth and financial sectors are working together here. On the flip side, Materials was the only sector in the red, down 0.45 percent. Industrials basically flat at negative 0.07 percent. Real Estate was the second-weakest performer at plus 0.17 percent. So we’re seeing a pretty broad-based rally, but the leadership is clearly in Financials and Tech.

Why are Financials outperforming? Look at the yield curve. The ten-year Treasury is now at 4.46 percent, up four basis points from where it was on May 12th. The two-year is at 4.0 percent, up five basis points. That steepening — the curve widened by two basis points — is actually good news for banks. When the long end rises faster than the short end, banks can earn wider spreads on their lending. And with CPI coming in hotter than expected at 3.95 percent year-over-year, up 63 basis points from the prior reading, there’s a sense that inflation isn’t going away quietly. That’s supporting yields, which supports financials. Tech’s strength likely reflects the fact that we’re still in a world where growth stories matter, and the Trump-Xi summit happening right now in Beijing is creating some optimism around trade negotiations and potential tariff truces. If tariffs get extended or negotiated down, that’s a win for tech supply chains.

Let’s talk about the volatility picture because this is crucial for you as an options trader. IV rank is sitting at 100 right now. That’s the highest possible reading. Your IV percentile is 22.43, which means we’re in the 22nd percentile of all historical IV readings for SPY. So yes, IV rank is maxed out, but that’s a bit of a quirk in how the metric works — it’s measuring where current IV sits relative to the past year’s range, and we’ve been in a pretty low volatility regime. The VIX itself is at 17.95, which is genuinely low. Current IV on the June 12 options is 16.75. This is important: if you’re thinking about selling premium, this is not an ideal environment. IV rank at 100 sounds juicy, but the absolute level of volatility is still compressed. You’re not getting paid a ton to take on risk. If you’re a buyer of premium, you’re getting a decent deal, but the market isn’t pricing in much uncertainty. The call-put IV skew is interesting too — ATM calls are at 16.75 IV while ATM puts are at 13.66 IV. That’s a meaningful gap, suggesting the market is slightly more concerned about downside than upside, but not dramatically so.

For price levels, you want to watch 745 as immediate support — that’s where we closed today and it’s also where the bulk of call open interest sits. Below that, 743 is a secondary support with 1,070 contracts of open interest on the puts. On the upside, 747 is the next resistance level with decent call volume at 72 contracts. The fifty-two-week high is 743.91, which we’ve basically already broken through, so we’re in fresh territory here. The fifty-two-week low is 575.60, so we’re well above that, but that’s your long-term floor if things go sideways.

Looking at the calendar, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in seven days on May 21st — that’s medium importance. More importantly, the Fed meeting is June 10th, which is twenty-seven days away. That’s your big event. Kevin Warsh just won Senate confirmation as the next Fed chair, which happened yesterday, so there’s some uncertainty around how policy might shift under new leadership. The market is clearly watching the Trump-Xi summit happening right now in Beijing — traders are pricing in a potential tariff truce extension and maybe some Boeing aircraft purchases. That’s a near-term catalyst that could move things in the next forty-eight hours depending on what gets announced.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV rank at 100 is a trap — the absolute level of volatility is still low, so selling premium isn’t as attractive as the headline number suggests. If you’re building a position this week, consider buying call spreads or put spreads to define your risk while the market is grinding higher, but don’t get cute trying to sell naked premium into what looks like a low-volatility environment.

So here’s the thing that caught my attention: Teresa Torres, who’s basically the godmother of structured product discovery, just spent a sprint actually coding to build AI-generated Opportunity Solution Trees. And she’s not an engineer—she’s a PM. That’s the whole story right there, and it matters for how you think about your roadmap.

Let me break down what’s actually happening here. An Opportunity Solution Tree is this framework where you map customer problems, then brainstorm opportunities to solve them, then explore different solutions. It’s incredibly useful but also incredibly time-consuming—you’re basically doing a bunch of structured thinking work that historically required workshops, sticky notes, and a lot of facilitation. Now Torres built tooling to let AI help generate these trees. The implication? You can run discovery cycles way faster, which means you can test more hypotheses before you commit engineering resources. Think about your next quarterly planning—instead of one or two deep-dive discovery sessions, you could potentially run five or six lightweight discovery sprints, each one giving you different angles on the same problem space.

Here’s where this gets practical for your SPY options trading mindset. You know how you’re thinking about implied volatility and probabilities? Discovery is the same thing. Right now, you’re probably making bets on which features matter based on gut feel or a handful of user interviews. But what if you could rapidly generate ten different problem framings, run them past your users, and actually measure which ones resonate? You’d have better odds. You’d be reducing your uncertainty before you spend months building something. That’s risk management applied to product.

The other thing Torres is showing us is that the PM role is changing. She coded this herself because she understood exactly what she needed—she wasn’t waiting for an engineer to build it. You don’t need to become a software engineer, but you do need to be comfortable enough with tools (whether that’s AI, no-code platforms, or basic scripting) that you can prototype your ideas. It’s like learning options Greeks so you can actually trade instead of just reading about it. The competency isn’t “be a coder.” It’s “be able to validate your thinking quickly without waiting for someone else.”

So the actionable piece: pick your next big discovery problem. Instead of running one big workshop, try using an AI tool to generate multiple framings of the opportunity. Prompt it properly—be specific about your customer segment and the context. Then take those generated trees, pick the top three, and validate them with actual users. You’ll move faster, you’ll have more data points, and you’ll make better bets on what to build next.

Summary

Americas: Traders are now pricing in two-to-one odds that U.S. inflation hits 4.5% or higher this year, with nearly 40% probability it breaks above 5 percent. This is a significant repricing of Fed pause expectations and suggests markets are bracing for stickier price pressures than recent data implied. Alright, imagine you’re playing a video game and your character has five different stats that determine how powerful you are in different situations. One stat makes you run faster, one makes you jump higher, one makes you stronger when you’re tired, one makes you better in the rain, and one makes you better when you’re older. Each stat does something different, and you need to understand all five to know if your character is going to win the game you’re about to play. That’s basically what the Greeks are in options trading. They’re five different measurements that tell you how your option contract is going to behave under different market conditions. Delta tells you how fast your option moves when the stock moves. Gamma tells you how much faster it moves as things change. Theta tells you how much your option loses value just from time passing by, like milk expiring in the fridge. Vega tells you how much your option cares about volatility, which is basically how crazy and jumpy the market is acting. And Rho tells you how much your option cares about interest rates. Each one is like a different superpower your option has, and understanding them means you stop being surprised when your trade doesn’t behave the way you expected.

Now let’s connect this to what’s actually happening in the market right now. Today SPY is sitting at 739.58, and we’re looking at June 12th calls, which gives us 30 days until expiration. The at-the-money call at the 740 strike has a delta of 0.53. Delta, in one sentence, tells you how many cents your option will move for every dollar the stock moves. So that 0.53 delta call means if SPY jumps up five bucks, you’d expect that call to gain about 2.65 dollars in value, give or take. It’s your option’s sensitivity to stock price movement. Gamma is the rate of change of delta itself, meaning as SPY keeps moving up, that delta doesn’t stay at 0.53—it gets bigger, accelerating your gains. Right now with IV at 16.25 and IV rank at a sky-high 100, we’re in a compressed volatility environment, which means gamma is relatively tame because the market isn’t expecting huge swings. Theta is the time decay, and here’s the real kick: every single day that passes, that call loses value just because the calendar flipped. With 30 days to expiration, theta is eating away at your position slowly, but it accelerates as you get closer to expiration date. Vega measures how much your option cares about IV changes. Today’s IV rank at 100 is telling us we’re at the high end of the historical IV range, which means vega is a double-edged sword—if IV drops, your call gets crushed even if SPY stays flat. And Rho, the least talked about Greek for short-dated options, tells you how sensitive your option is to interest rate changes, which matters more on longer-dated contracts but is basically background noise on a 30-day call.

Here’s what this actually means for you as a trader looking at the board right now. You’re looking at that 740 call with 0.53 delta, and you’re thinking, “Okay, so I’ve got a coin-flip probability that this expires in the money.” But delta isn’t probability—it’s just the rate of change. The real story is that with IV rank at 100, you’re in a volatility compression zone. That means vega is working against you if you’re long calls. The market is saying, “Volatility can’t go much higher,” which means if you buy that call today expecting a volatility expansion to bail you out, you’re probably going to be disappointed. Instead, you need the stock to actually move. The gamma on this 0.53 delta call is modest because we’re in a low-IV environment, so your gains won’t accelerate dramatically on a big move—they’ll be steady and linear. That’s actually not terrible if you’re confident in direction, but it’s not explosive. The theta is working against you every single day—you’re bleeding money just from time passing. On a 30-day option, you’re probably losing somewhere around 0.03 to 0.05 per day on that call, which adds up fast if SPY doesn’t move. So if you’re thinking about buying calls here, you need to have a conviction that SPY is going higher in the next week or two, not just someday before June 12th. If you’re thinking about selling calls, you’re getting paid that theta decay, which is beautiful—time is on your side. You could sell that 740 call, pocket the premium, and if SPY stays between 735 and 745, you win. The Greeks aren’t just academic—they’re telling you right now whether you should be a buyer or a seller, and with theta and vega positioned the way they are today, the sellers have the edge.

Alright, let’s talk about what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 739.58, up just 0.19 percent on the day. Now, I know that sounds like a nothing burger, but context matters here. We’re up about eight dollars from last Thursday’s close of 731.58, so we’ve had a solid week-long grind higher. Today was basically a consolidation day after that 0.83 percent jump on Friday. The market is catching its breath, which is actually healthy. Volume came in at about 14 million shares, which is pretty normal for a Wednesday. So we’re not seeing capitulation or panic buying, just steady accumulation.

Now let’s dig into what actually moved the needle today, because the sector breakdown tells us a lot about investor psychology right now. Technology, the XLK, led the charge with a 0.38 percent gain. That’s the biggest mover on the upside and it’s basically carrying the entire index. Consumer Discretionary was up 0.26 percent, Communication Services up 0.16 percent. Those are all pretty modest, but they’re all positive. Here’s where it gets interesting though. On the downside, Utilities absolutely got hammered, down 1.36 percent. Real Estate fell 1.01 percent. Financials dropped 1.1 percent. And Energy slid 0.68 percent. So we’ve got a real divergence here. The growth-oriented, higher-beta sectors are winning, while the defensive, rate-sensitive sectors are getting sold. That’s a very specific market message.

Why is this happening? Well, it all comes back to inflation and what it means for interest rates. This morning we got the CPI print and it was hot. Headline CPI came in at 3.8 percent year-over-year, which is above the forecast of 3.5 percent. Core CPI, which strips out food and energy, came in at 2.8 percent, also above expectations of 2.7 percent. Now, I want to give you a quick primer on how this feeds into the broader picture because it matters for your options positioning. CPI is the consumer price index, right? It measures what regular people are paying for stuff. But there’s another inflation gauge that comes first in the month, and that’s the Producer Price Index, or PPI. PPI measures what producers and businesses are paying for raw materials and inputs. It’s like the canary in the coal mine for CPI. When PPI rises, it usually means CPI will follow a few weeks later because those costs get passed down the supply chain. So traders are watching PPI very closely as a leading indicator. Today’s hot CPI print suggests that the inflation story isn’t cooling as fast as the Fed would like, and that’s spooking the bond market and anything that benefits from lower rates. Utilities and Real Estate are rate-sensitive because they’re often financed with debt, so when rates go up, their financing costs go up and their valuations compress. Meanwhile, tech is benefiting because investors are rotating into growth stories that can actually grow their way out of this inflation problem, rather than sitting in a utility that just pays a dividend while rates rise.

Let’s talk about volatility because this is crucial for your options strategy. IV rank is sitting at 100, which is the highest possible reading. That means implied volatility is at the 100th percentile of its 52-week range. The current IV is 16.25, and the VIX closed at 18.06. Now, what does this mean for you as an options trader? This is a premium seller’s dream. When IV rank is at 100, you’re getting paid maximum dollars to sell options. If you’re thinking about selling calls or puts, or running a strangle or a straddle, the premiums are juicy right now. The flip side is that if you’re a buyer, you’re paying peak prices. So if you’re thinking about buying protection or buying directional calls, you’re overpaying. The market is pricing in elevated uncertainty, which makes sense given that inflation is hotter than expected and we’ve got the Fed meeting coming up in 28 days. That’s a lot of time for things to move around.

Let’s talk about the technical levels you need to watch. SPY’s 52-week high is 740.79, and we’re sitting at 739.58, so we’re literally one dollar away from all-time highs. That’s your immediate resistance. If we break through 740.79, the next level to watch is probably 745, which is a psychological round number and a level we haven’t tested yet this year. On the downside, support is at 735.47, which was today’s low. If we break below that, the next meaningful support is probably 730, which was roughly where we were last week. The 50-day moving average is probably somewhere around 735 to 737, so that’s another level to keep an eye on.

Now, what’s coming in the next 48 hours that could move this market? Tomorrow, May 14th, we’re getting the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update. That’s a medium-importance data point, but given that mortgage rates have been climbing—the 30-year is at 6.37 percent, up from 6.30 percent last week—this could add to the narrative that rates are sticky. That could put more pressure on Real Estate and Financials. Beyond that, the big event is the Fed meeting on June 10th, which is 28 days away. That’s not imminent, but it’s close enough that traders are starting to price in expectations for what the Fed might do. Right now, the market is pricing in maybe one rate cut by the end of the year, but that could change if inflation stays elevated. There are no major earnings reports in the next seven days, so we’re in a relatively quiet period from a corporate perspective.

Here’s my one-sentence takeaway for you: With IV rank at 100 and tech leading while defensive sectors lag, this is a good environment to sell premium on weakness, but don’t get caught holding short puts if we break below 735 because the inflation narrative could flip the market’s mood quickly.

Here’s what’s happening in product right now, and it’s worth paying attention to. There’s this creeping narrative that to be a real product manager, you need to be able to code, design, or basically do everyone else’s job. Teresa Torres and Petra Wille just called this out hard on their podcast, and honestly, it’s the most useful thing I’ve heard in weeks.

Let me give you the practical angle first. When you’re running a mature product—especially one dealing with complex user behavior and market signals like SPY options traders—your superpower isn’t being able to jump into the codebase and ship a feature. It’s knowing what to build and why it matters to your users. Torres actually spent time doing an engineering sprint herself to build AI-generated Opportunity Solution Trees, and you know what she discovered? That hands-on experience was valuable for understanding constraints, but it didn’t make her a better PM. It actually distracted her from what she’s genuinely exceptional at: structuring discovery and turning chaos into clarity.

The real issue is this: if you spend your time being a mediocre engineer or designer, you’re not being an excellent strategist. Your senior traders don’t need you to write their data pipeline—they need you to understand why they’re abandoning your platform at 2 PM on earnings days, or why they’re not using your IV rank alerts even though you built them. That requires a completely different skill set. It requires you to sit with users, ask the right questions, and resist the urge to just build your way out of a problem.

Here’s where it connects to something deeper: The Pragmatic Engineer piece revisits the “no silver bullets” concept, and the takeaway is sharp. AI isn’t going to solve your core PM job any more than learning Python will. There’s no shortcut to understanding your market. You still need to talk to traders, watch how they use your product, and figure out where the real friction is. The tools change—maybe you use AI to synthesize discovery notes faster, or to spot patterns in user behavior—but the fundamental work of being a product manager hasn’t changed in 40 years.

So what should you actually be doing? First, get comfortable with your role’s actual boundaries. You don’t need to be a developer, but you do need to understand technical feasibility well enough to have smart conversations. Second, invest heavily in discovery and user research. That’s where the leverage is. When you’re deciding between building a new Greeks calculator or improving order flow visualization, the answer lives in your users’ workflows, not in your ability to code it up. Third, recognize that the “product builder” trend is partly a confidence crisis—companies are asking PMs to do everything because they haven’t figured out how to structure teams properly. Don’t fall for it.

The concrete application: if you’re managing a trading product, spend next week doing the opposite of what the hype suggests. Don’t try to learn options Greeks or build a prototype. Instead, sit with five power users for an hour each and ask them about their biggest friction points. Record it. Transcribe it. Look for patterns. That’s the work that actually moves the needle. Your developers will respect you more for bringing clarity than for being a mediocre coder. And your traders will definitely prefer a PM who understands their pain over one who’s distracted trying to be an engineer.

Imagine you’re at a carnival, and there’s a game where you can win prizes by guessing if a balloon will float higher or lower than a certain point. You pay a small fee to make your guess, and if you’re right, you win a toy. If you think the balloon will stay somewhere in the middle, you can place a bet on that too. You can even place bets on both sides at the same time, covering all your bases, hoping to win no matter where that balloon goes. This way, you enjoy the thrill of the game without worrying too much about which way the balloon floats.

Now, let’s connect that carnival game to the world of trading. In options trading, an Iron Condor is like placing those bets on both sides of the balloon. Instead of guessing just one outcome, you’re selling two spreads at once, hoping the price of SPY doesn’t get too wild. Let’s say SPY is currently at $735.22, and you think it will stay within a certain range. You might sell a lower spread, betting it won’t drop below $730, and a higher spread, betting it won’t rise above $740. This gives you a chance to collect the premiums from both trades as long as SPY stays between those two prices, just like collecting prizes if the balloon stays in the middle.

Now, knowing how Iron Condors work can really change how you look at your trades. Today, SPY is trading at $735.22, with an implied volatility (IV) of 17.79, which is at a 100% rank. This means the market is expecting significant price movements soon, making it a prime time for an Iron Condor strategy. Higher IV typically means you can collect more premium when selling options, so you might consider setting your strikes at $730 and $740. Since SPY’s price is fluctuating a bit, you can look at the current trend and see if the recent high was $737.22 and the low was $734.66. If you believe SPY will hover around $735 in the next month, you can set up your Iron Condor to maximize your potential profit while minimizing risk, as long as the market stays calm. Just remember, the beauty of this strategy is that you’re taking advantage of the premium from both sides while betting on stability. So, as you think about your next move, keep an eye on that IV and the overall market sentiment, and you might just find the right moment to deploy your Iron Condor for some solid returns.

In a recent podcast episode featuring Ryan Nystrom from Notion, he dives into the concept of spec-driven development, which is a game-changer for product managers like you. Imagine this: instead of getting bogged down in the nitty-gritty of coding, you can focus on the strategic aspects of your project while AI handles the implementation. Nystrom explains how this workflow allows for automating mundane tasks, such as daily standups and even shipping pull requests with just a single comment. This means you can free up your time to think critically about the product rather than getting lost in technical details.

A practical takeaway is to start integrating AI tools that can assist your development team. For instance, consider using AI to generate code snippets based on your specifications. This kind of automation not only speeds up the development process but also ensures that your team spends more time on innovation rather than repetitive tasks. Nystrom emphasizes that by embracing these tools, you can create an environment where your engineers act more like ‘agents’ who are empowered to make decisions rather than just following orders.

Additionally, Nystrom highlights the importance of clear and concise specifications. As a PM, you should work on crafting high-quality specs that outline what you want the product to achieve. This clarity helps the AI systems to generate accurate code that aligns with your vision. If you’re unsure how to write effective specs, consider collaborating with your engineering team to gather insights on what information is most useful for them.

Another point he makes is the value of feedback loops. As you implement this workflow, make sure to establish regular check-ins with your team to evaluate how well the AI is performing and whether the specifications are being met. This iterative process can help you refine your approach and ensure that both the AI and your team are aligned on project goals.

Finally, don’t shy away from experimenting with these new tools. Start small by introducing one or two AI-driven processes into your workflow and gauge the results. This hands-on experimentation will not only enhance your understanding of AI capabilities but also help you build a culture of innovation within your team. By adopting a spec-driven development approach, you’re not just keeping up with the trends; you’re setting your product up for success in a rapidly evolving tech landscape.
